rulings.Case Title: CONSTANCE BOURGES VS. DANY SCHER et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion For Pltf'S Vacate Judgment Deft'S 4th Demurrer With Leave To Amend Complaint/Show Burden Of Proof For Ccp 473 (B) Excusable Negligence /Timeliness Issue- Accept 5th Amended Complaint
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Thursday, June 25, 2015, Line 1, PLAINTIFF CONSTANCE BOURGES' Notice Of Motion For Pltf'S Vacate Judgment Deft'S 4th Demurrer With Leave To Amend Complaint/Show Burden Of Proof For Ccp 473 (B) Excusable Negligence /Timeliness Issue- Accept 5th Amended Complaint. The motion is denied. There is no good cause to vacate the judgment. The court denies defendant's sanctions request. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order with the name and address of the prevailing party's counsel or the prevailing party if pro per in the top left of the first page of proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/SRB)

rulings.Case Title: HEIDI DENTON et al VS. KWAN SHIU YAN FUNG et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Plaintiffs Heidi Denton And Gaydean Valdez'S Motion To Compel Defendant Kwun Shiu Fung To Provide Further Responses To Form Interrogatories; Request For Admission; And To Appear For Deposition With Documents; And For Sanctions
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Tuesday June 23, 2015, Line 5, Plaintiffs Heidi Denton And Gaydean Valdez's Motion To Compel Defendant Kwun Shiu Fung To Provide Further Responses To Form Interrogatories; Request For Admission; And To Appear For Deposition With Documents; And For Sanctions. Pro Tem Judge Steven Stein, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Hearing required. The Court will set deposition dates to take place within the discovery cut-off. The parties shall "meet and confer" prior to the hearing to attempt to reach agreement on the definition of the "incident" which is the subject of the written discovery disputes. The Court will hear oral argument and then rule on all written discovery objections. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to sbs@knoxricksen.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: CHLOE GATES et al VS. THOMAS A. BROCATO, INDIVIDUALLY AND AS TRUSTEE OF et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Plaintiffs Chloe Gates, Michael Isgar And Michael Mullan'S Motion To Compel Further Responses To Special Interrogatories To Thomas Brocato, Set One, And Request For Sanctions Against Defendants And Defendants Counsel, Jane Corning
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Tuesday June 23, 2015, Line 6, Plaintiffs Chloe Gates, Michael Isgar And Michael Mullan's Motion To Compel Further Responses To Special Interrogatories To Thomas Brocato, Set One, And Request For Sanctions Against Defendants And Defendants Counsel, Jane Corning. Pro Tem Judge Steven Stein, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Plaintiff's motion to compel a further response to Special Interrogatories is granted in part and denied in part. The special interrogatories seeking identification of all residential properties managed or owned by the defendant is denied. The query is overbroad in that it seeks identification of properties which may not be covered by the San Francisco Rent Control Ordinance or other laws. It also seeks financial information which invade the defendant's privacy rights. When privacy rights are involved the information sought must be directly relevant to one of the issues in the action. The prospect an inquiry might lead to admissible evidence is insufficient. Board of Trustees v. Superior Court (1981) 119 CA3d 516, 525. The motion is granted as to Interrogatory 27. The defendant shall provide a code complaint response which in the Court's discretion is amended to read: "Describe in detail the specifics of what defendant plans to repair or build at the subject property." Moving party's request for sanctions is denied. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to sbs@knoxricksen.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: CRAIG BIRD VS. THE LOVE SHACK COOPERATIVE, INC., A CALIFORNIA ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Plaintiff Birds Motion For Leave Of Court To Withdraw Admissions
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Tuesday June 23, 2015, Line 4, PLAINTIFF CRAIG BIRD'S Motion For Leave Of Court To Withdraw Admissions. Pro Tem Judge Steven Stein, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Plaintiff's motion to amend or withdraw his responses to Requests for Admissions 14, 15 and 16 is granted based on the error of counsel which is substantiated by said counsel's declaration under oath. See CCP. Code § 2033.300. That plaintiff responded to Form Interrogatory 17.1 for each admission confirms the admission was due to an editing error. The defendant shall be permitted to pursue additional discovery related to the matters involved in the withdrawn admissions. The costs of additional discovery shall be borne by the moving party. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to sbs@knoxricksen.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/JPT)
